title: Verathon Prime Plus Bladder Scanner - Touchscreen / Display Issues
description: Structured Clinical Engineering steps for troubleshooting touchscreen and display issues on the Verathon Prime Plus bladder scanner, focusing on external checks before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Power Check
steps.instructions:
Confirm the device is fully charged or connected to AC power.
Verify the battery status indicator is normal.
Why: A low battery or intermittent power can cause touchscreen responsiveness issues or blank screens.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions:
Check for loose or damaged AC adapters, docking station connectors, and probe connections.
Re-seat all cables securely.
Why: Loose connections can prevent the display from initializing correctly.

steps.title: Clean the Touchscreen
steps.instructions:
Use a soft, lint-free cloth dampened with water or approved screen cleaner.
Avoid abrasive materials or excessive moisture.
Why: Oils, dirt, or debris can block touch input or create ghost touches.

steps.title: Restart the Device
steps.instructions:
Power off completely, wait 10–15 seconds, then power on.
Observe the display during boot for abnormalities.
Why: Temporary software glitches often resolve with a restart.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage
steps.instructions:
Inspect the touchscreen for cracks, deep scratches, or liquid ingress.
Why: Physical damage can permanently affect touchscreen performance or display visibility.

steps.title: Test in Alternate Settings
steps.instructions:
Use a different AC outlet or battery pack if available.
Try a known-good docking station or cable.
Why: Confirms whether the issue is with the scanner or its external power interface.

steps.title: Soft Reset / Factory Reset (if applicable and safe)
steps.instructions:
Only perform if safe and documented according to manufacturer guidelines.
Why: Corrupted settings can cause display or touch issues.

steps.title: If the Problem Persists
steps.instructions:
Common external causes have been ruled out.
The device should be removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Verathon-approved repair or internal bench evaluation.
Note: Knowing when to stop is proper troubleshooting; avoid internal repairs beyond your scope.

CCR.Complaint:
Touchscreen is unresponsive or display intermittently blank.
Example: “Display freezes when attempting to select patient profile; touchscreen does not respond to input.”
CCR.Cause:
Observed: Device fully charged, cables intact, screen clean, but unresponsive; no physical damage noted.
Example: Suspected internal touchscreen controller or display issue after external checks.
CCR.Resolution:
Action taken: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for manufacturer evaluation.
Example: “Prime Plus scanner returned to Biomedical Engineering for vendor repair; patient scanned using alternate device.”

helpfulDetails: Battery charge level at the time of issue
Any recent drops or liquid exposure

title: Verathon Prime Plus Bladder Scanner – Inaccurate Bladder Volume Readings
description: Structured Clinical Engineering guide for troubleshooting inaccurate bladder volume readings on the Verathon Prime Plus scanner, focusing on external, easily verifiable causes.

steps.title: Check Battery and Power Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is fully charged or connected to power. Low battery can affect probe signal processing.

steps.title: Verify Probe and Cable Connection
steps.instructions:
Inspect the ultrasound probe for secure connection to the scanner.
Look for visible damage to the probe cable or connector.

steps.title: Inspect Probe Surface
steps.instructions:
Check for cracks, debris, or dried gel on the probe face.
Clean gently with approved disinfectant wipes.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Patient Positioning
steps.instructions:
Patient should be supine, bladder midline, legs slightly bent.
Inaccurate readings often occur if patient is leaning or moving.

steps.title: Ensure Adequate Ultrasound Gel Application
steps.instructions:
Apply sufficient gel to allow full contact between probe and skin.
Air gaps reduce signal quality and volume accuracy.

steps.title: Check Device Settings
steps.instructions:
Verify correct gender selection (male/female) and patient type (adult/pediatric).
Ensure the scanner mode is set to “Bladder Scan” and not other preset modes.

steps.title: Perform a Test Scan on a Known Volume Phantom (if available)
steps.instructions: Confirms the scanner and probe are reading correctly outside of patient variability.

steps.title: Repeat Scan Carefully
steps.instructions:
Perform 2–3 consecutive scans, noting any variability.
Small movements or probe angle changes can affect readings.

steps.title: If the Problem Persists
steps.instructions:
External causes have been ruled out (positioning, gel, settings, probe condition).
The device should be removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for Verathon service or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint:
Reported inaccurate bladder volume readings.
Example: “Scanner reads 150 mL when catheterization shows 300 mL.”
CCR.Cause:
Observed misalignment of probe, insufficient gel, or device settings mismatch.
Example: “Probe was not centered on bladder; patient slightly rotated.”
CCR.Resolution:
Actions taken to correct: checked connections, cleaned probe, reapplied gel, repeated scan.
Example: “Scanner cleaned, settings verified, repeated scan with proper positioning; readings remained inconsistent, device removed from service and labeled OOS.”

helpfulDetails: Number of scan attempts and variability
Final device status (e.g., OOS, sent for service)

title: Verathon Prime Plus Bladder Scanner – Probe / Transducer Error
description: Structured Clinical Engineering troubleshooting for probe or transducer errors on the Verathon Prime Plus bladder scanner.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions: Turn the scanner off, wait 10–15 seconds, and turn it back on. Rationale: Clears temporary software glitches that may trigger false probe errors.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Cable
steps.instructions: Check for visible cuts, kinks, or frayed wires along the cable. Ensure connectors are fully seated in the scanner port. Rationale: Loose or damaged cables are the most common cause of transducer errors.

steps.title: Clean the Probe and Connector
steps.instructions: Use manufacturer-approved cleaning wipes or solutions. Remove any gel residue or debris from the probe tip and connector pins. Rationale: Dirt or residual gel can interfere with ultrasound signal transmission.

steps.title: Swap the Probe (If Available)
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good probe to the scanner. Rationale: Confirms whether the error is with the probe or the scanner itself.

steps.title: Verify Software / Firmware Status
steps.instructions: Check the device screen for firmware version and error logs. Apply any manufacturer-recommended updates if safely possible. Rationale: Some probe errors may be triggered by outdated software.

steps.title: Check Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the scanner is not exposed to extreme temperatures or moisture. Rationale: Ultrasound transducers can be sensitive to environmental conditions.

steps.title: If the Problem Persists
steps.instructions: All external and easily verifiable causes have been ruled out. Label the device Out of Service. Remove it from patient care and escalate for vendor repair or bench evaluation. Knowing when to stop and escalate is a key part of proper troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: Device reports “Probe Error” or “Transducer Error.” Example: “User reported intermittent probe error during catheter volume scan.”
CCR.Cause: Observed issues with probe cable, connectors, or probe contamination. Example: “Probe connector pins were dirty, or cable showed minor fraying.”
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned probe and connector, reseated cable, power-cycled device. Tested with a known-good probe. Device returned to service or labeled Out of Service if error persisted. Example: “After cleaning and power cycle, error resolved with same probe. Device returned to service.”

helpfulDetails: Cable integrity check (any kinks or cuts)
Final device status (in service, out of service)

title: Verathon Prime Plus Bladder Scanner – Fails to Power On / Shuts Off
description: Structured Clinical Engineering approach to troubleshoot a Verathon Prime Plus bladder scanner that does not power on or shuts off unexpectedly.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Is Safe & Device Is Not In Use
steps.instructions: If the scanner is in active use on a patient, finish the exam safely or switch to a standby device before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify External Power Source
steps.instructions: If connected to AC power, confirm the outlet has power using a known working device or outlet tester. Inspect the power adapter for correct model, visible damage, frayed wires, bent pins, or debris. Ensure the adapter is fully seated in both the device and the wall outlet/UPS/power strip.

steps.title: Check Battery and Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: If operating on battery, remove the battery module and inspect contacts for corrosion, dirt, or misalignment. Reinsert the battery making sure it locks into place. If a spare battery is available, try swapping to determine if the battery is the cause.

steps.title: Examine Power Button & Physical Interface
steps.instructions: Press and hold the power button a few seconds. Confirm the button isn’t stuck or physically damaged. Observe any LED indicators or display backlight activity when pressing power.

steps.title: Look for Power Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: If lights flicker briefly or the screen attempts to light then shuts down, this may suggest insufficient power from either the battery or adapter.

steps.title: Test With Minimal Connections
steps.instructions: Remove any unnecessary peripherals (printers, carts with other equipment, docking stations) and try powering on with just the essential power connection.

steps.title: Observe for Overheating or Unusual Symptoms
steps.instructions: Feel for excessive heat at the power supply, battery, or scanner casing. Listen for unusual sounds (buzzing, fan noise) that could indicate internal issues.

steps.title: Basic Reset (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Some devices have a reset function (consult quick start/reference sheet only — do not open the device). Perform a reset if documented and external.

steps.title: If the Problem Persists
steps.instructions: After completing the above external checks (power source, battery, cables, power switch, basic reset), internal causes remain likely. At this point: Remove the device from clinical service. Label the device Out of Service. Send for manufacturer service or internal bench evaluation. Do not attempt internal board repairs or disassembly beyond what the user manual specifies for battery and cables.

CCR.Complaint: Bladder scanner fails to power on or shuts off shortly after powering.
CCR.Cause: Observed no power at outlet with tester; power adapter barrel connection loose; battery contacts showed corrosion; device attempt to start but immediately loses power.
CCR.Resolution: Verified outlet with tester; replaced power adapter with known good unit; cleaned battery contacts; confirmed device powers on and remains stable. End user tested functionality and confirmed normal operation. (If the device still fails, note that it was taken out of service and refer to manufacturer service in the resolution.)

helpfulDetails: Power adapter model and whether swapped
LED or display behavior during power attempts
Any signs of physical damage, unusual heat, or sounds

title: Verathon GlideScope Video Laryngoscope – Video Display Not Turning On / Screen Blank
description: Troubleshooting a GlideScope video laryngoscope when the video display fails to power on or remains blank, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge
steps.instructions: Ensure the battery is fully charged or connected to a reliable power source. A depleted or improperly seated battery is a common cause for a blank screen.

steps.title: Verify Battery Seating and Contacts
steps.instructions: Remove and reinsert the battery. Inspect contacts for dirt, corrosion, or misalignment. Clean gently if necessary.

steps.title: Confirm Power Switch Operation
steps.instructions: Press and hold the power button per manufacturer guidance. Look for any indicator lights on the handle or display. No lights may indicate power or battery issues.

steps.title: Inspect Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the blade, handle, and display connections are secure. Wiggle connectors gently to verify firm contact. Loose cables can prevent video output.

steps.title: Check Display Brightness and Screen Settings
steps.instructions: If the unit powers on but shows a black screen, adjust brightness or input source settings. Some models have a standby mode—toggle standby/power to test.

steps.title: Swap Components if Available
steps.instructions: If another GlideScope handle or battery is available, swap to isolate the issue. If the display works with a different handle or battery, the original component may be defective.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for cracks, impact damage, or liquid exposure on handle or screen. Damage may prevent operation and requires service.

CCR.Complaint: GlideScope screen remains blank, unit will not power on.
CCR.Cause: Battery was tested and seated correctly, power button responded, cables were connected, but display remained blank.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to Biomedical Engineering for bench evaluation.

title: Verathon GlideScope Blade Camera / Image Quality Issues
description: Guide for troubleshooting poor, blurry, distorted, or no image on GlideScope video laryngoscope blades.

steps.title: Check the Power and Monitor
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is powered on and fully charged (if battery-operated) or connected to power. Confirm the monitor input matches the GlideScope source. Why: Image issues can appear if the monitor is not functioning properly.

steps.title: Inspect the Blade Lens
steps.instructions: Examine the blade tip lens for debris, condensation, blood, or coating residue. Clean gently using a soft, lint-free cloth with manufacturer-approved disinfectant. Why: Even a small smudge can blur or obscure the image.

steps.title: Check the Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Verify the blade-to-handle connection is secure; remove and reconnect if loose. Inspect the cable for kinks, frays, or visible damage. Why: Poor connection or damaged cable can cause intermittent or distorted video.

steps.title: Test With Another Compatible Blade
steps.instructions: Swap the suspected blade with a known-working blade on the same handle and monitor. Why: Helps determine if the issue is blade-specific or handle/monitor-related.

steps.title: Test With Another Monitor
steps.instructions: If available, connect the same blade and handle to a different monitor. Why: Confirms whether the monitor itself is causing image issues.

steps.title: Check the Environment
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is not exposed to extreme lighting conditions that can affect camera visibility.

CCR.Complaint: GlideScope blade shows blurry, distorted, or no image.
CCR.Cause: Observed smudged lens, loose connection, or tested blade confirmed the image issue.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned lens, secured connections, tested with known-good blade and monitor. If problem persists, removed from service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service if unresolved

title: Verathon GlideScope Handle or Control Buttons Unresponsive
description: Troubleshooting guide for unresponsive handle or control buttons on a Verathon GlideScope video laryngoscope, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge
steps.instructions: Ensure the handle battery is fully charged. Low or depleted batteries are a common cause of unresponsive buttons. Swap with a known-good battery if available.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts and Connections
steps.instructions: Remove the battery and inspect the contacts for corrosion, dirt, or damage. Clean contacts with a lint-free cloth if dirty. Reinsert battery and test functionality.

steps.title: Examine Handle for Visible Damage
steps.instructions: Look for cracks, moisture, or other physical damage to the handle or buttons. Damage may cause intermittent or complete unresponsiveness.

steps.title: Check Control Cable / Module Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the handle is properly seated in the display module or docking station. Disconnect and reconnect to confirm secure connection. Loose or partially seated handles may prevent button input registration.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions: Turn off the display module or laryngoscope system, wait 10 seconds, then power on. Some unresponsiveness can be cleared by a simple reboot.

steps.title: Verify Software / Firmware Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the display module firmware is up to date. Some button responsiveness issues may be firmware related. Consult the device service records or manufacturer’s update schedule.

CCR.Complaint: Handle buttons not responding during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Battery contacts were dirty; secure connection restored only temporarily.
CCR.Resolution: Battery contacts cleaned; if problem recurs, handle sent out for repair.

title: Verathon BladderScan i10 Bladder Scanner - Poor bladder detection or inaccurate volume reading
description: Troubleshooting poor bladder detection, low-confidence scans, or bladder volume readings that appear inconsistent with patient condition.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot scan accuracy on an active clinical concern without involving the care team. Action: Confirm the patient is clinically stable and that staff are not relying on a questionable bladder volume reading for an urgent decision. If the reported volume does not match the patient’s condition, instruct staff to verify using another approved method or another bladder scanner. Do not keep repeating scans on the same patient if results are clearly unreliable. Expected outcome: Patient care decisions are not based on a suspected inaccurate scan. Why it matters: A poor bladder scan can lead to delayed catheterization, unnecessary catheterization, or incorrect assessment of urinary retention.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is poor bladder detection, inconsistent readings, or clearly inaccurate volume values. Action: Ask staff what they observed: No bladder detected. Low-confidence scan. Very different readings on repeated scans. Volume does not match clinical signs. Scanner displays positioning or aiming guidance repeatedly. Issue occurs with one patient or multiple patients. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is identified before assuming a device problem. If the problem only occurred once or on a difficult patient scan, the issue may be technique, anatomy, positioning, or patient condition rather than device failure.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Active Use for Testing
steps.instructions: Take the BladderScan i10 out of patient care workflow before evaluating it. Action: Clean the device per facility procedure. Move it to a safe area for inspection and functional checks. Use another bladder scanner for patient care while the issue is being evaluated. Expected outcome: The device can be checked without delaying care or contaminating equipment.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe and Scanner Exterior
steps.instructions: Check for obvious external damage. Action: Inspect the probe face for: Cracks. Scratches. Cloudiness. Dried gel. Residue. Impact damage. Loose housing. Liquid intrusion. Inspect the scanner body, touchscreen, charging contacts, and any connected accessories. Expected outcome: The scanner and probe should be clean, intact, and free of visible damage. If the probe face is cracked, damaged, contaminated, or physically compromised, remove the device from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Cleaning of the Probe Face
steps.instructions: A dirty or damaged probe surface can interfere with ultrasound transmission. Action: Clean the probe face using approved cleaning methods for the facility and manufacturer. Remove dried gel, disinfectant residue, tape residue, or debris. Allow the probe to dry if cleaning solution is present. Expected outcome: The probe surface is clean and smooth before retesting. If cleaning resolves the poor detection or inconsistent readings, return the device to service and document the cause.

steps.title: Check Ultrasound Gel Use
steps.instructions: Poor gel contact is one of the most common external causes of poor bladder detection. Action: Confirm staff are using enough approved ultrasound gel. Check whether the gel is dried out, expired, contaminated, or being applied too thinly. Verify gel is placed between the probe and patient skin with no air gaps. Expected outcome: The probe should have full acoustic contact with the patient during scanning. If proper gel use restores consistent readings, educate staff and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Scan Technique and Probe Placement
steps.instructions: Incorrect probe angle or placement can produce poor detection or inaccurate volume readings. Action: Confirm staff are scanning in the correct lower abdominal location. Verify the probe is aimed toward the bladder and not too high, too low, or off-center. Review whether the scanner’s on-screen aiming guidance is being followed. Repeat a scan using proper positioning if Clinical Engineering is trained and authorized to perform a non-patient functional check. Expected outcome: The scanner should guide the user toward an acceptable bladder image and produce repeatable results when used correctly. If readings improve with corrected technique, the issue is likely user technique, not device failure.

steps.title: Consider Patient-Related Factors
steps.instructions: Some patients are harder to scan accurately. Action: Ask whether the issue occurred on a patient with: Obesity. Abdominal dressings. Surgical wounds. Ascites. Pregnancy. Abdominal fluid collections. Recent surgery. Patient movement. Inability to lie still. Very low bladder volume. Expected outcome: Patient-specific factors are identified as possible causes of poor detection. If the device works normally on other patients or test conditions, document that no device fault was confirmed.

steps.title: Compare Repeatability
steps.instructions: Check whether the reported readings are repeatable. Action: Have staff perform multiple scans using proper technique, if clinically appropriate. Compare whether values are reasonably consistent or widely different. Check whether the scanner repeatedly asks for repositioning or shows poor confidence. Expected outcome: A properly functioning scanner should provide reasonably consistent results under similar scan conditions. If readings vary wildly despite good technique, clean probe surface, proper gel, and appropriate patient conditions, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Battery Level and Power Stability
steps.instructions: Low battery or unstable power may contribute to poor device behavior, freezing, or incomplete scans. Action: Check the battery charge level. Place the unit on the approved charger or cart dock if battery is low. Confirm the scanner charges normally. Allow adequate charge time before retesting. Expected outcome: The scanner should operate normally with sufficient battery power. If poor scanning only occurs at low battery levels, document the battery behavior and evaluate battery/runtime separately.

steps.title: Restart the Scanner
steps.instructions: Software or temporary scan processing issues may clear after a restart. Action: Power the BladderScan i10 off. Wait briefly. Power it back on. Allow it to fully boot. Repeat a basic functional check. Expected outcome: The touchscreen and scan workflow should respond normally after restart. If restart resolves the issue and readings are consistent, return the device to service after documenting the reset and successful test.

steps.title: Check for Screen Prompts, Error Messages, or Software Warnings
steps.instructions: The scanner may provide guidance that helps identify the cause. Action: Look for messages related to: Probe connection. Scan quality. Aiming. Battery. Storage. Software update. System error. Calibration or service condition. Expected outcome: Any displayed message should be documented exactly as shown. If a persistent system, probe, calibration, or service message appears, remove the device from service and escalate.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Exam Workflow Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Incorrect patient or scan workflow can create documentation confusion or perceived inaccurate results. Action: Confirm staff are using the correct scan mode, patient selection workflow, and result save process. Check whether the reported “wrong result” is actually a documentation, upload, or patient association issue. Expected outcome: The displayed scan result and saved result should match the intended patient/workflow. If the scanner reads correctly but results are being assigned, saved, or uploaded incorrectly, troubleshoot the connectivity/documentation workflow instead.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Scanner if Available
steps.instructions: A comparison can help separate device failure from patient or technique issues. Action: Use another verified bladder scanner on the same type of scenario if clinically appropriate. Compare the results only as a general troubleshooting reference, not as a diagnostic decision by Clinical Engineering. Expected outcome: If another scanner consistently detects the bladder while the BladderScan i10 does not, the i10 may require service evaluation. If both devices struggle, the issue may be patient condition, technique, or scan environment.

steps.title: Review Recent History
steps.instructions: Recent drops, cleaning events, software issues, or charging problems may explain the failure. Action: Ask staff whether the scanner was recently: Dropped. Exposed to fluid. Cleaned with excess liquid. Left off charger. Updated. Moved between departments. Used after a long period of storage. Check work order history for repeat complaints. Expected outcome: Repeated or event-related failures are identified. If the issue started after physical damage or fluid exposure, remove the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the Verathon BladderScan i10 was giving inconsistent bladder volume readings and frequently failed to detect the bladder.
CCR.Cause: Probe face was found with dried gel residue, and repeat testing showed improved scan consistency after cleaning and proper gel application.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned probe surface, verified adequate gel use, restarted the unit, performed functional scan workflow check, and returned the BladderScan i10 to service.

helpfulDetails: Battery level at time of complaint
Any displayed warnings or error messages
Whether another scanner was used for comparison
Whether issue occurred on one patient or multiple patients
Recent drop, fluid exposure, or cleaning event

title: Verathon BladderScan i10 Bladder Scanner - Probe Not Recognized or Probe Communication Error
description: Troubleshooting probe recognition, probe communication, scan failure, or scanner-to-probe connection issues on the Verathon BladderScan i10.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot probe communication issues during an active patient scan if results may be needed for care decisions. Remove the BladderScan i10 from clinical use if the probe is not recognized, scan cannot start, or communication errors repeat. Use another bladder scanner or approved clinical assessment method if bladder volume information is needed. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed or based on an unreliable scan attempt. Why it matters: A bladder scanner that cannot communicate with the probe may fail to acquire valid images or may interrupt the exam.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact behavior before assuming probe or scanner failure. Power on the BladderScan i10. Check whether the device displays a probe communication error, probe not detected message, scan unavailable condition, or missing probe indicator. Ask clinical staff when the issue occurs: at startup, when attempting to scan, after the probe was dropped, after cleaning, after charging or cart docking, or intermittently during use. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is confirmed and documented. If the probe is recognized after restart and scanning works normally, document the temporary communication issue and return the device to service only after functional testing.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe and Cable Externally
steps.instructions: Check for obvious physical damage. Inspect the probe housing for cracks, impact marks, loose seams, damaged lens surface, fluid intrusion, or missing labels. Inspect the cable, strain relief, and connector area for cuts, kinks, crushed sections, exposed conductors, or looseness. Expected outcome: Probe and cable appear physically intact. If physical damage, fluid intrusion, or cable strain relief damage is found, remove the unit from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check the Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the probe is properly connected to the scanner or cart interface as applicable. Power the unit off. Disconnect and reconnect the probe connection fully. Ensure the connector is seated straight and secure. Do not force the connector if resistance is abnormal. Power the unit back on and check whether the probe is recognized. Expected outcome: The scanner detects the probe and allows scanning. If the issue resolves, perform a test scan or approved functional check, document the reseated connection, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Connector Pins and Contact Surfaces
steps.instructions: Look for external connector problems that could block communication. Inspect the probe connector and scanner-side connector for bent pins, debris, corrosion, moisture, gel, dried cleaner, or damaged contacts. Use only approved cleaning methods for external surfaces. Allow any recently cleaned parts to dry completely before retesting. Expected outcome: Connectors are clean, dry, undamaged, and able to make reliable contact. If pins are bent, contacts are damaged, or corrosion is present, remove the device from service for repair.

steps.title: Confirm the Probe Is Correct for the Scanner
steps.instructions: Make sure the connected probe belongs with the BladderScan i10 system. Check that the probe is a compatible Verathon BladderScan i10 probe. Verify the probe has not been swapped from another model or system. Compare asset tags or department labels if multiple bladder scanners are in use. Expected outcome: The scanner and probe are correctly matched. If the probe is mismatched, reconnect the proper probe and retest.

steps.title: Restart the Scanner
steps.instructions: Clear a possible temporary software or communication lockup. Power the BladderScan i10 completely off. Wait briefly. Power it back on. Allow the system to fully boot before attempting a scan. Expected outcome: The scanner initializes normally and recognizes the probe. If the issue resolves, complete a functional check and document the restart.

steps.title: Check Battery and Power Status
steps.instructions: Low or unstable power can contribute to startup or accessory communication issues. Confirm the scanner has adequate battery charge. Connect the unit to its charger or docking station if available. Verify charging indicators or power status. Retest probe recognition after the unit has stable power. Expected outcome: The scanner powers normally and maintains probe communication. If the unit only recognizes the probe while connected to power, document battery or power-related behavior and evaluate the battery/charging system separately.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Cleaning-Related Issues
steps.instructions: Probe communication problems may occur after cleaning if moisture enters or remains around the connector. Ask whether the issue started after cleaning or disinfection. Inspect for wet connector areas, residue, or excessive liquid exposure. Allow the device and probe exterior to dry fully before reconnecting. Expected outcome: No moisture or residue is present near the probe connection. If the issue resolves after drying, educate staff on proper cleaning precautions and document the finding.

steps.title: Test With Known-Good Accessories if Available
steps.instructions: Use substitution to isolate the issue without opening the device. If your facility has another compatible BladderScan i10 probe or scanner, test the suspected probe on a known-good scanner or test a known-good probe on the affected scanner. Follow facility policy for accessory swapping and asset tracking. Expected outcome: If the suspected probe fails on another scanner, the probe is likely faulty. If a known-good probe fails on the affected scanner, the scanner-side connection or internal electronics may be faulty. If both work after reconnection, the issue may have been a seating or contamination problem. If substitution identifies a faulty component, remove the affected component from service and send for repair or replacement.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Scan Check
steps.instructions: Do not return the scanner to service based only on the error disappearing. Perform an approved test scan or functional verification according to department practice. Confirm the probe is recognized, scan initiates normally, image acquisition proceeds, and the result displays without communication errors. Expected outcome: The BladderScan i10 completes a scan without probe recognition or communication errors. If the scan completes normally and the issue does not recur, return the device to service and document the verification.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Verathon BladderScan i10 displayed a probe communication error and would not start a bladder scan.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the probe connector was not fully seated and had dried gel residue around the connector area.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned the external connector area, reseated the probe, restarted the scanner, completed a functional scan check, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Battery level at time of failure
Whether issue occurred at startup or during scan
Presence of gel, cleaner residue, moisture, or corrosion
Whether known-good probe or scanner was tested
Whether the device was returned to service or sent for repair

title: Verathon BladderScan i10 Bladder Scanner - Battery Not Charging or Short Runtime
description: Troubleshooting charging failure, short battery runtime, docking issues, power adapter problems, or battery status concerns on the BladderScan i10.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot charging or runtime issues while the device is needed for active patient care. Remove the BladderScan i10 from clinical use if battery power is unreliable, the device shuts down unexpectedly, or the battery status is unclear. Use another bladder scanner if scans are needed. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on a scanner that may lose power during use. Why it matters: A bladder scanner that powers off mid-scan can delay assessment, documentation, or catheterization decisions.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Charging or Runtime Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm what clinical staff are actually seeing. Ask whether the issue is device will not charge, device only charges intermittently, battery drains quickly, device shuts off during use, charging icon does not appear, or device only works while docked or plugged in. Check the battery icon or charge status on the display if the device powers on. Expected outcome: The complaint is clearly identified before replacing accessories or escalating for repair. If the issue was due to misunderstanding normal battery status or charge indication, educate staff and return the device to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Source
steps.instructions: Check the wall outlet before assuming a charger or device failure. Verify the charger or dock is plugged into a known working hospital-grade outlet. Test the outlet with another device or outlet tester if available. Avoid using loose outlets, damaged receptacles, extension cords, or questionable power strips. Expected outcome: The charging setup has confirmed incoming AC power. If the outlet is dead or loose, move the charger to a working outlet and notify Facilities if needed.

steps.title: Inspect the AC Power Adapter and Cord
steps.instructions: Look for obvious external damage. Check the power adapter for cuts or frayed cable insulation, bent connector, loose strain relief, cracked power brick, burn marks, unusual heat, or electrical smell. Confirm the adapter is fully seated into the charging dock or device connection point. Expected outcome: The power supply and cord appear safe and connected. If the adapter or cord is damaged, remove it from service and replace it with an approved Verathon-compatible power supply.

steps.title: Check the Charging Dock or Charging Base
steps.instructions: If the i10 uses a dock or charging cradle, verify the device is seating correctly. Place the scanner firmly into the dock. Check for rocking, poor alignment, debris, damaged contacts, or anything preventing full seating. Remove any labels, tape, covers, or added accessories that may interfere with docking. Expected outcome: The scanner sits securely and begins charging. If reseating the device restores charging, allow it to charge and verify normal runtime before returning it to service.

steps.title: Inspect Charging Contacts and External Surfaces
steps.instructions: Dirty or obstructed contacts can prevent charging. Inspect the scanner and dock contacts for dust, gel residue, dried cleaning solution, corrosion, bent or recessed contacts, or physical damage. Clean only according to approved hospital and manufacturer cleaning practices. Do not scrape contacts or use excessive liquid. Expected outcome: Charging contacts are clean, dry, and unobstructed. If cleaning restores charging, document the finding and return the unit after confirming stable charging.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charge Time
steps.instructions: A deeply discharged battery may need time before normal operation returns. Connect the scanner to a verified charger or dock. Allow the device to charge undisturbed. Check whether the battery icon, charge indicator, or power status changes after a reasonable charging period. Expected outcome: Battery level increases and the device powers on normally. If the scanner begins charging normally, continue charging and verify runtime before returning to the department.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Charging
steps.instructions: Charging may fail only when the cable, dock, or scanner is moved. While the device is safely off patient use, gently observe the charging indicator while lightly repositioning the scanner in the dock or moving the power cord. Do not flex damaged cables or force connectors. Expected outcome: Charging remains stable without flickering or dropping out. If charging starts and stops with movement, suspect a loose dock, damaged cable, poor contact, or internal charging port issue.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Charger or Dock If Available
steps.instructions: Use substitution to isolate the fault. Try the scanner on a known-good compatible charger or dock. If available, try a known-good BladderScan i10 on the suspected charger or dock. Expected outcome: If the scanner charges on another dock, the original dock or power supply is likely faulty. If another scanner charges on the original dock, the scanner itself may have a battery or internal charging issue. If neither device charges, suspect the charger, dock, cable, or outlet. If substitution identifies a bad external accessory, replace the accessory and verify charging.

steps.title: Evaluate Runtime After a Full Charge
steps.instructions: Short runtime should be verified before assuming battery failure. Fully charge the scanner using a verified charger. Power the device on and observe whether the battery drains abnormally fast during normal use or standby. Ask staff whether the scanner is being left off the charger between uses or stored unplugged for long periods. Expected outcome: Runtime is consistent with normal clinical use. If runtime is still short after a full charge, the battery may be degraded or the device may have an internal power issue.

steps.title: Check for Heat, Odor, Swelling, or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Battery or charging problems with physical symptoms should not be ignored. Inspect the device and dock for unusual heat, burning smell, cracked housing, swollen battery area, liquid intrusion, drop damage, or signs of corrosion. Expected outcome: No visible safety concerns are present. If heat, odor, swelling, liquid damage, or electrical damage is found, remove the device from service immediately and do not continue charging attempts.

steps.title: Verify Final Device Status
steps.instructions: Before returning the scanner to service, confirm the charging issue is resolved. Confirm device powers on normally, battery level increases while charging, charging indicator is stable, scanner remains powered during basic operation, no unusual heat, smell, or physical damage is present, and dock and power supply are safe to use. Expected outcome: The BladderScan i10 charges reliably and has acceptable runtime for clinical use. If the issue is resolved, document the work order and return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the Verathon BladderScan i10 would not hold a charge and powered off shortly after removal from the charging dock.
CCR.Cause: Verified outlet power and charger connection; scanner charged intermittently due to poor seating and debris on the dock contact area.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned exterior charging contact area per approved procedure, reseated scanner in dock, confirmed stable charging indicator and normal power-on operation.

helpfulDetails: Battery icon or displayed charge level
Whether charging was intermittent or completely failed
Whether another charger or dock was tested
Any unusual heat, odor, swelling, or liquid damage
Environmental factors such as storage location, unplugged dock, cleaning residue, or liquid exposure

title: Verathon BladderScan i10 Bladder Scanner - Wireless/cart connection or results upload failure
description: Troubleshooting BladderScan i10 wireless, cart, docking, or results upload issues caused by power, connection, network, or workflow problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot connectivity or upload issues while delaying urgent patient care. Action: Confirm the scan result has been reviewed by clinical staff before troubleshooting upload problems. If the result is needed immediately, have staff document the result manually according to facility policy. Do not remove the scanner from use during active patient workflow unless another bladder scanner is available. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed because of a documentation or upload failure. Why it matters: Connectivity issues usually affect documentation, but patient care decisions may still depend on the displayed scan result.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is with the wireless connection, cart connection, docking station, printer, network upload, or EMR/interface transfer. Action: Ask staff what failed: Scanner will not connect to cart. Scanner will not connect to Wi-Fi. Results do not upload. Results remain queued. Patient/result data does not appear in the expected system. Upload works intermittently. Scanner loses connection after docking or moving rooms. Check whether the device displays an error message, connection icon, upload status, or queued result indicator. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before parts or network issues are assumed.

steps.title: Confirm the Scanner Has Adequate Battery Power
steps.instructions: Low battery or unstable power can interrupt wireless communication and upload functions. Action: Check the battery level on the BladderScan i10. Place the device on its approved charger or cart/dock if the battery is low. Allow the scanner to charge long enough to rule out low-power behavior. Expected outcome: The scanner has enough power to maintain communication and complete uploads. If the issue resolves after charging, return the device to service and document low battery as the likely cause.

steps.title: Check the Cart, Dock, and Charging Contacts
steps.instructions: If the issue involves cart communication, docking, or upload after docking, inspect the external connection points. Action: Confirm the scanner is seated correctly on the cart or charging dock. Remove and reseat the scanner. Inspect the charging/dock contacts for gel, residue, damage, bent pins, or debris. Clean external contact areas according to facility-approved cleaning procedure. Check whether the cart has power and whether any indicator lights are present. Expected outcome: The scanner docks normally and any cart-based communication or charging indicator behaves as expected. If reseating or cleaning the contacts restores connection, stop troubleshooting and document the correction.

steps.title: Restart the Scanner and Cart Components
steps.instructions: A temporary software or communication lockup may prevent upload or connection. Action: Power the scanner off normally. Restart the cart or connected accessory if applicable. Power the scanner back on and allow it to fully boot. Reconnect to the cart, dock, or wireless network. Attempt a test upload or check whether queued results begin transferring. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software lockup clears after restart. If upload resumes after restart, document the reset and monitor for repeat failures.

steps.title: Verify Wireless Network Availability
steps.instructions: Wireless upload failures may be caused by local Wi-Fi coverage, network outage, or device roaming issues. Action: Check whether other wireless medical devices in the area are communicating normally. Move the BladderScan i10 to an area with known good wireless coverage. Confirm the wireless icon or connection status improves. Ask clinical staff whether the issue occurs in one room, one unit, or across the facility. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to device-specific failure, location-based signal issue, or larger network problem. If the scanner uploads successfully in another area, document possible local wireless coverage or network access issue.

steps.title: Confirm the Device Is Using the Correct Network Profile
steps.instructions: The scanner may fail to upload if it is not connected to the correct hospital wireless profile or configured upload destination. Action: Check the device’s network connection status if accessible through the approved user or service configuration menu. Confirm the device is not connected to a guest network, wrong SSID, or non-clinical network. Verify whether the device recently moved between departments, buildings, or network zones. Do not change advanced network settings unless authorized by facility procedure. Expected outcome: The device is confirmed to be on the intended clinical network or identified as needing IT/network support.

steps.title: Check for Queued or Failed Results
steps.instructions: The scanner may be collecting results but failing to transmit them. Action: Review the results/upload queue if available. Look for pending, failed, or unsent results. Attempt upload again while connected to known good wireless coverage or cart/dock connection. Confirm whether new results fail, old queued results fail, or all results fail. Expected outcome: Upload failure is confirmed as isolated to specific results, all results, or the upload pathway. If queued results upload successfully after reconnecting, return the device to service and document the recovery.

steps.title: Confirm Patient/Operator Workflow Requirements
steps.instructions: Some upload failures occur because required patient, operator, or encounter information is missing. Action: Ask clinical staff whether the scan was associated with the correct patient or encounter. Confirm whether operator login, patient selection, barcode scan, or required fields were completed before upload. Check whether the issue happens only when scanning without patient selection or when using manual entry. Expected outcome: Upload failure is not caused by incomplete workflow or missing required documentation fields. If completing the required workflow allows upload, educate staff as appropriate and document the finding.

steps.title: Test With a Known Good Workflow
steps.instructions: Use a controlled test to separate scanner failure from workflow or network failure. Action: Use a known working cart, dock, network area, and approved test workflow if available. Perform a non-patient test according to facility policy. Confirm whether the device connects and uploads under controlled conditions. Expected outcome: The scanner either uploads successfully in a known-good setup or continues to fail despite external causes being ruled out. If it uploads successfully, the original issue is likely location, workflow, or network related.

steps.title: Escalate to IT or Interface Support When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the scanner connects to Wi-Fi but results do not appear in the destination system, the issue may be outside the scanner. Action: Confirm whether the scanner shows upload complete or upload failed. If upload appears successful on the device but results are missing from the EMR, middleware, or documentation system, contact the appropriate IT/interface team. Provide device identifier, time of scan, patient/account details if allowed by policy, location, and upload status. Expected outcome: The issue is routed correctly instead of replacing scanner hardware unnecessarily.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Connection Failure Persists
steps.instructions: If the scanner repeatedly fails to connect, upload, or communicate after basic checks, remove it from clinical use. Action: Label the device Out of Service. Include the charger, cart, dock, and any related accessories if they may be part of the issue. Send the unit for bench evaluation or vendor/service review. Expected outcome: A device with unreliable documentation or connectivity is removed until the fault is resolved.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Verathon BladderScan i10 was scanning normally but results were not uploading from the cart/wireless connection.
CCR.Cause: Scanner had pending results and poor wireless connection in the reported room; upload completed when tested in an area with known good wireless coverage.
CCR.Resolution: Verified scanner power, reseated scanner on cart, restarted unit, confirmed wireless connectivity, completed test upload, and returned device to service.

helpfulDetails: Scanner battery level at time of failure
Whether results were queued or failed
Whether upload failed in one room or multiple areas
Whether other devices had network issues
Whether the result appeared in the destination system

title: Verathon BladderScan i10 Bladder Scanner - Touchscreen, Boot, or Software Freeze Issue
description: Troubleshooting touchscreen lockups, startup freezes, delayed booting, or software response issues on the BladderScan i10.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the bladder scanner while it is actively being used for a patient scan. Action: Remove the BladderScan i10 from patient use if the screen is frozen, the system will not boot properly, or scan results cannot be trusted. Use another bladder scanner if a bladder volume measurement is clinically needed. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on equipment that may be unresponsive or unreliable. Why it matters: A frozen or partially booted bladder scanner may display old data, fail to save results, or provide unreliable workflow information.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the user experienced. Action: Ask clinical staff what happened: Was the touchscreen not responding? Did the unit freeze during startup? Did it freeze during a scan? Did it freeze while printing, documenting, or saving results? Did the screen go blank? Was there an error message? Power the unit on and observe the behavior. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before assuming a software or hardware issue. If the device boots normally and the touchscreen responds, document that the issue was not duplicated and continue functional checks.

steps.title: 3. Check Battery Charge and Power Status
steps.instructions: Low battery or unstable power can cause startup or software behavior that looks like a freeze. Action: Place the BladderScan i10 on its charging base or connect it to its approved power source. Verify that charging indicators appear. Allow the unit to charge if the battery is very low. Attempt to power the unit on again. Expected outcome: The device boots normally and the touchscreen responds. If the issue resolves after charging, return the device to service after confirming normal operation and documenting the low battery condition.

steps.title: 4. Inspect the Charging Base and Power Supply
steps.instructions: A poor charging connection may leave the battery too depleted for normal startup. Action: Check that the power supply is connected firmly to the wall outlet and charging base. Inspect the power cord for damage, bent connectors, loose strain relief, or signs of overheating. Test the wall outlet or move the charger to a known-good outlet. Confirm the scanner sits correctly on the charging base. Expected outcome: The unit charges consistently and powers on normally. If the scanner does not charge or repeatedly loses power, troubleshoot as a power or charging issue rather than a software issue.

steps.title: 5. Perform a Normal Restart
steps.instructions: A temporary software lockup may clear with a controlled restart. Action: Power the unit off using the normal power button method if the touchscreen allows it. Wait briefly. Power the unit back on and observe startup. Expected outcome: The BladderScan i10 completes startup and responds normally. If the issue resolves, perform a basic scan workflow check before returning the device to service.

steps.title: 6. Check for Touchscreen Contamination or Interference
steps.instructions: Fluids, gel, gloves, cleaning residue, or screen protectors can interfere with touchscreen response. Action: Inspect the screen for ultrasound gel, disinfectant residue, adhesive, cracks, or moisture. Clean the touchscreen using facility-approved cleaning methods. Dry the screen completely. Remove any loose or damaged screen protector if applicable and approved by facility practice. Retest touchscreen response. Expected outcome: Touch input responds accurately after the screen is clean and dry. If touchscreen response returns to normal, document contamination or residue as the likely cause.

steps.title: 7. Check for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Screen or housing damage may cause intermittent touchscreen failure or boot problems. Action: Inspect the scanner for: Cracked touchscreen. Damaged housing. Liquid intrusion signs. Loose charging contacts. Impact damage. Unusual heat. Unusual smell. Loose probe or cable connection, if applicable. Expected outcome: No visible damage is found. If damage, liquid exposure, heat, or odor is present, remove the device from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 8. Verify the Unit Is Not Stuck During Startup
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue occurs before or after the operating screen loads. Action: Power cycle the device and observe where startup stops: Blank screen. Logo screen. Loading screen. Login or user screen. Scanning screen. Results screen. Note whether the screen brightness changes, buttons respond, or audible tones occur. Expected outcome: The point of failure is identified for repair documentation. If the unit repeatedly freezes at the same point during startup, suspect a software, memory, battery, or internal hardware issue.

steps.title: 9. Check for Accessory or Docking-Related Problems
steps.instructions: Some lockups may occur when the device is docked, charging, printing, exporting, or communicating with another system. Action: Test the scanner off the charging base if battery level allows. Then test it on the charging base. If the issue occurs only while docked, inspect the dock contacts and charging connection. If the issue occurs during printing, export, or documentation transfer, note that behavior separately. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to the scanner, charging base, or communication/documentation workflow. If removing the device from the dock restores normal touchscreen use, inspect the dock and power setup further.

steps.title: 10. Confirm Date, Time, User, and Workflow Screens Respond
steps.instructions: Software lockups may appear only during certain workflow steps. Action: After startup, confirm the touchscreen responds on normal menu screens. Check whether the scanner can move through: Home screen. Patient or operator entry. Scan setup. Scan acquisition. Results screen. Documentation or print/export screen, if used. Expected outcome: The device responds throughout the normal clinical workflow. If freezing occurs only at one workflow point, document the exact screen and action that triggers the freeze.

steps.title: 11. Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Do not return the device based only on a successful restart. Action: Complete a basic operational check per facility practice. Confirm: Unit powers on normally. Touchscreen responds. Battery status is acceptable. Scanner does not freeze during menu navigation. A scan can be initiated. Results screen displays normally. Charging status is normal. Expected outcome: The BladderScan i10 operates normally through the expected workflow. If all checks pass, return the device to service.

steps.title: 12. Do Not Attempt Deep Internal Repair During Floor Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Avoid opening the unit or attempting board-level troubleshooting during normal clinical response. Action: Do not disassemble the scanner on the unit. Do not attempt internal software recovery unless trained and authorized. Do not clear or reset configuration settings without knowing the impact on clinical workflow. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting remains safe, controlled, and appropriate for Clinical Engineering response.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Verathon BladderScan i10 touchscreen froze during startup and would not allow a bladder scan to be started.
CCR.Cause: Verified the unit had a very low battery and would not fully boot until placed on a known-good charging base.
CCR.Resolution: Charged the unit, confirmed normal startup, verified touchscreen response, completed a functional scan workflow check, and returned the device to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue occurs docked or undocked
Whether issue occurs during scan, results, print, or export

title: Verathon BladderScan BVI 9400 Bladder Scanner - Inaccurate or Inconsistent Bladder Volume Readings
description: Troubleshooting inconsistent bladder volume readings caused by positioning, scanning technique, probe issues, settings, patient factors, or device condition.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not delay urinary assessment or treatment if the patient’s clinical condition does not match the scanner reading. Remove the BladderScan BVI 9400 from clinical use if readings are clearly unreliable, erratic, or do not match patient symptoms. Have clinical staff verify the patient condition using another scanner, catheterization, or approved clinical method if needed. Expected outcome: Patient care decisions are not based on a scanner that may be reporting inaccurate volume. Why it matters: An inaccurate bladder volume can lead to delayed catheterization, unnecessary catheterization, or missed urinary retention.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is truly inconsistent readings or a scanning technique concern. Ask clinical staff what they are seeing: Readings vary widely between repeated scans; Scanner reports very low volume despite suspected retention; Scanner reports high volume unexpectedly; Bladder not centered during scan; Scan quality indicator is poor; Issue occurs with all users or only some users. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is understood before assuming a device problem. If the issue is clearly user- or technique-related, provide education and retest before removing the device from service.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Patient and Probe Positioning
steps.instructions: Check that scanning technique is appropriate. Confirm the probe is placed over the lower abdomen, aimed toward the bladder, and held steady during the scan. Verify adequate ultrasound gel is used. Repeat the scan after repositioning the probe slightly and rescanning. Expected outcome: Readings become more consistent when probe placement and scan angle are corrected. Why it matters: Bladder scanners are highly dependent on probe angle, patient anatomy, gel contact, and proper targeting. If readings stabilize after proper positioning, return the device to service.

steps.title: Check for Patient Factors That Can Affect Readings
steps.instructions: Rule out clinical conditions that may cause inaccurate bladder volume estimates. Ask staff if the patient has factors such as: Obesity or difficult anatomy; Ascites; Pregnancy; Abdominal wounds or dressings; Pelvic masses; Recent bladder surgery; Patient movement during scan; Very low bladder volume. Expected outcome: Clinical staff understand that some patients may produce less reliable scan results. Why it matters: The device may be functioning normally but limited by patient condition or ultrasound visibility.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Face and Cable
steps.instructions: Check the probe for obvious external damage. Inspect the probe face for cracks, dents, residue, dried gel, or contamination. Inspect the probe cable for cuts, kinks, strain, loose connection, or intermittent movement-related faults. Clean the probe according to facility procedure and manufacturer-compatible disinfectant guidance. Expected outcome: Probe surface is clean, intact, and properly connected. If damage is found, remove the device from service and send for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify the Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the probe is fully connected to the scanner. Power the unit off. Check that the probe connector is secure and not loose or visibly damaged. Reconnect the probe if safe and appropriate. Power the unit back on and repeat a test scan. Expected outcome: Scanner recognizes the probe normally and produces stable readings. If the probe connection is loose, damaged, or intermittent, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Check Battery and Power Condition
steps.instructions: Verify that low battery or unstable power is not affecting operation. Check battery status on the unit. Place the scanner on the charging base or connect it to the correct charging setup. Allow adequate charge time if the battery is low. Repeat testing after the battery level is acceptable. Expected outcome: Scanner operates normally without low-power behavior, shutdowns, freezing, or inconsistent performance. If the battery will not hold charge or the unit behaves erratically, remove it from service for battery or power evaluation.

steps.title: Check Device Settings and User Selection
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct operating mode or patient setting is being used if applicable. Confirm staff are using the appropriate scan mode or patient selection options available on the device. Check whether a setting was changed before the issue started. Compare readings using the same patient position and scan method. Expected outcome: Readings improve when correct scan settings and consistent technique are used. Why it matters: Incorrect mode selection or inconsistent setup can create repeatability problems.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Repeatability Check
steps.instructions: Test whether the device produces repeatable results under controlled conditions. Have the same trained person perform multiple scans on the same patient or approved test condition. Keep patient position, probe location, gel amount, and scan angle as consistent as possible. Record the displayed volumes and whether the bladder targeting indicator appears centered. Expected outcome: Repeated scans should be reasonably consistent when technique and conditions are controlled. If readings swing widely despite controlled technique, the issue may be device- or probe-related.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Bladder Scanner if Available
steps.instructions: Use a known-good scanner for comparison. Scan the same patient with another verified bladder scanner using the same general technique. Compare the results. Expected outcome: The suspect BVI 9400 should produce readings in a similar clinical range. If the BVI 9400 is consistently far off compared with a known-good scanner, remove it from service.

steps.title: Restart the Unit and Recheck
steps.instructions: Clear possible temporary software or startup issues. Power the unit off. Wait briefly. Power it back on and allow it to complete startup normally. Repeat the scan test. Expected outcome: The device starts normally and readings become consistent. If the device freezes, fails startup, shows errors, or continues giving erratic readings, remove it from service.

steps.title: Check for Calibration, Self-Test, or Service Due Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for device messages that suggest service is required. Review the display for calibration prompts, startup errors, self-test failures, or service warnings. Document any message exactly as displayed. Expected outcome: No service-related warning is present. If the unit displays a calibration, self-test, or service failure message, remove it from service and send for bench evaluation or vendor service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Verathon BladderScan BVI 9400 was giving inconsistent bladder volume readings during repeated scans.
CCR.Cause: Probe face and cable were inspected, battery charge was verified, and repeated scans remained inconsistent compared with a known-good bladder scanner.
CCR.Resolution: Device was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation and probe/calibration assessment.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, including any startup, self-test, calibration, or service messages
Accessories swapped, including another probe, charger, charging base, or comparison bladder scanner if available
Power behavior, including battery level, charging status, shutdowns, freezing, or low-power behavior
Environmental factors, including whether the issue occurred on one patient or multiple patients, patient anatomy, dressings, movement, or gel contact
Indicator lights, scan quality indicators, targeting indicators, or display messages observed during testing
Final device status, including whether the scanner was returned to service or removed from service and labeled Out of Service

title: Verathon BladderScan BVI 9400 Bladder Scanner - Probe scan button not responding
description: Troubleshooting a Verathon BladderScan BVI 9400 when the probe scan button does not start a scan.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the scanner while it is being used for an active patient assessment. Action: Remove the BVI 9400 from patient use if the scan button is not responding. Provide the clinical team with another bladder scanner if one is available. Expected outcome: Patient care continues without relying on a scanner that may not capture a valid scan. Why it matters: A nonresponsive scan button can delay bladder volume assessment or lead staff to repeat scans unnecessarily.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is truly the probe scan button or a broader system problem. Action: Power on the BVI 9400. Confirm the display turns on normally. Attempt to start a scan using the probe scan button. Observe whether: The button does nothing. The scanner freezes. The scanner gives an error. The button works intermittently. The console controls respond normally. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed and separated from a general power, touchscreen, or startup issue. If the probe button works during testing, document “could not duplicate,” clean and inspect the device, and return to service only if operation is reliable.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge and Power Status
steps.instructions: Low battery or unstable power can cause unreliable button response or incomplete operation. Action: Check the battery level on the scanner. Place the unit on its charger or connect the proper charging setup. Allow the battery to charge if low. Retest the probe scan button after adequate charge is available. Expected outcome: The scan button responds normally once stable power is restored. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the battery/power condition.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe and Button Area
steps.instructions: The scan button may be physically stuck, damaged, contaminated, or worn. Action: Inspect the probe housing. Check the scan button for: Cracks. Fluid residue. Cleaning chemical buildup. Sticky movement. Loose or sunken button feel. Physical impact damage. Press the button several times and confirm whether it moves normally. Expected outcome: The button should move freely and return to its normal position. If the button is physically damaged, sticky, or does not return normally, remove the unit from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Clean the Probe Exterior
steps.instructions: Dried gel, disinfectant residue, or contamination around the button can interfere with normal movement. Action: Clean the probe exterior according to facility-approved cleaning procedures. Pay close attention to the area around the scan button. Do not allow fluid to enter openings or seams. Allow the probe to dry fully. Retest the scan button. Expected outcome: The button responds if the issue was caused by external contamination or residue. If cleaning resolves the issue, stop and document the cleaning performed.

steps.title: Check Probe Cable and Strain Relief
steps.instructions: A damaged probe cable or loose internal conductor can make the scan button appear nonresponsive. Action: Inspect the probe cable from the probe head to the console connection. Look for: Cuts. Kinks. Crushed sections. Loose strain relief. Exposed wiring. Intermittent response when the cable is moved. Gently reposition the cable while testing the scan button. Expected outcome: The scan button should respond consistently without needing the cable held in a certain position. If button response changes when the cable is moved, remove the scanner from service for probe/cable repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Probe Connection at the Console
steps.instructions: The probe may not communicate properly if the connector is loose, contaminated, or not fully seated. Action: Power the unit off. Inspect the probe connection at the console. Confirm the connector is fully seated and secure. Check for bent pins, debris, corrosion, or visible connector damage if accessible externally. Power the unit back on and retest. Expected outcome: The probe button responds after the probe connection is confirmed secure. If the connector is damaged, do not force it. Remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Check for System Lockup or Software Freeze
steps.instructions: The probe button may be functional, but the scanner may not respond because the system is frozen. Action: Observe whether the display updates normally. Check whether other controls respond. Power the unit off and restart it. Allow the scanner to complete startup. Retest the probe button. Expected outcome: The probe button responds normally after restart if the issue was a temporary system lockup. If the scanner repeatedly freezes or the button stops responding again, remove it from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Scan Mode and Readiness
steps.instructions: The scanner may not respond if it is not in the correct state to begin a scan. Action: Confirm the scanner is fully powered on and ready to scan. Verify the unit is not still starting up, processing a prior scan, printing, charging-only, or displaying an unresolved error. Clear any completed scan workflow if appropriate. Retest the probe scan button from the normal scan-ready screen. Expected outcome: The probe button starts a scan when the system is in the correct operating state. If the scanner is not reaching a scan-ready state, troubleshoot the startup, self-test, or system error separately.

steps.title: Check for Liquid Intrusion or Physical Abuse
steps.instructions: A failed probe button can result from dropped equipment or fluid exposure. Action: Inspect the scanner and probe for: Cracked housing. Loose seams. Evidence of liquid entry. Unusual odor. Visible corrosion. Rattling parts. Impact marks. Ask clinical staff whether the unit was dropped, heavily cleaned, or exposed to fluids. Expected outcome: No signs of impact or fluid intrusion are present. If fluid intrusion or impact damage is suspected, remove the device from service and send for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Test Repeated Button Operation
steps.instructions: Intermittent button failures are still failures. Action: Perform several scan-button tests from the scan-ready screen. Do not use a patient for repeated troubleshooting scans. Confirm whether the button responds every time. Expected outcome: The scan button should operate consistently. If the button only works sometimes, remove the unit from service. Intermittent probe controls are not reliable for patient care.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Verathon BladderScan BVI 9400 probe scan button would not start a bladder scan.
CCR.Cause: Verified the unit powered on, but the probe scan button remained nonresponsive after battery charging, cleaning, cable inspection, and connector reseating.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the BVI 9400 from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for probe/button repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or any error messages displayed
Accessories swapped, including charger, probe connection, or approved charging setup
Power behavior, battery charge level, and whether the scanner powers on normally
Environmental factors such as cleaning residue, fluid exposure, impact damage, unusual smell, or heat
Indicator lights, display response, or whether other controls respond
Final device status, including whether the scanner was returned to service or removed from service

title: Verathon BladderScan BVI 9400 Bladder Scanner - Battery not charging or poor battery runtime
description: Troubleshooting charging failure, short battery runtime, docking issues, power adapter problems, or battery age concerns on the BladderScan BVI 9400.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the bladder scanner during active patient assessment if the device is unreliable. Action: Remove the BladderScan BVI 9400 from clinical use if it cannot power on, hold charge, or complete scans reliably. Use another bladder scanner if patient assessment is needed. Expected outcome: Patient care continues using a reliable device. Why it matters: A scanner with poor battery runtime may shut down during use, delay care, or cause repeat scanning.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Power Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact behavior before assuming battery failure. Action: Ask clinical staff what was observed: Device will not power on. Device powers on only while docked or plugged in. Battery does not charge. Battery drains quickly. Charging indicator does not appear. Device shuts off during scanning. Runtime is shorter than expected. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified. If the issue is confirmed, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: 3. Check the Charging Dock or Power Connection
steps.instructions: Verify that the charging station is receiving power. Action: Confirm the dock or charging base is plugged into a known working outlet. Check that the power supply is firmly connected to the wall outlet and to the charging base. Look for damaged power cords, bent connectors, loose strain reliefs, or missing power indicators. Expected outcome: The charger has stable power and no obvious external damage. If the outlet or power connection was the issue, correct it, verify charging begins, document the repair, and stop.

steps.title: 4. Test the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Rule out a simple facility power issue. Action: Plug another known-working device or outlet tester into the same receptacle. If the outlet is controlled by a wall switch, confirm it is switched on. Avoid using damaged outlets, loose receptacles, or questionable power strips. Expected outcome: The outlet provides reliable power. If the outlet is failed, move the charger to a working outlet or notify Facilities, document findings, and stop.

steps.title: 5. Inspect How the Scanner Sits in the Charging Base
steps.instructions: A poor physical connection can prevent charging. Action: Place the BVI 9400 properly into the dock or charging base. Confirm it seats fully and does not rock, sit crooked, or lose contact. Inspect the dock area for debris, tape, labels, gel residue, or anything blocking proper contact. Expected outcome: The scanner sits securely and charging status appears normally. If reseating the scanner resolves the charging issue, clean the dock area as appropriate, verify charging, document, and stop.

steps.title: 6. Inspect External Charging Contacts
steps.instructions: Dirty or damaged contacts can cause intermittent charging. Action: Inspect the scanner and dock charging contacts for contamination, corrosion, bent contacts, residue, or physical damage. Clean external contact areas only according to approved facility cleaning practices and manufacturer-safe methods. Do not scrape, bend, or aggressively clean contacts. Expected outcome: Contacts are clean, aligned, and free of visible damage. If cleaning resolves the issue, verify charging and runtime before returning the device to service.

steps.title: 7. Check the Battery Indicator Behavior
